recap: P(A | B) = P(A and B) / P(B). For without-replacement problems, update the totals after the first draw. Conditional probability means you only consider the outcomes where the given event occurred.

Q1.question: P(A and B) = 0.12 and P(B) = 0.4. Find P(A | B).

Q2.question: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue balls. One is drawn at random — it is red. What is the probability that a second draw (without replacement) is also red?

steps: After one red drawn: 4 red remain out of 7 total.

Q3.question: A class of 30 students: 18 play football (F), 12 play tennis (T), and 5 play both. A student is chosen at random. Given they play tennis, find the probability they also play football.

Q4.question: A bag has 4 white and 6 black counters. Two are taken without replacement. Find the probability that both are white.

Q5.question: A box has 3 defective and 7 good items. Two items are tested without replacement. Find the probability that at least one is defective.

steps: P(at least one defective) = 1 - P(both good).
P(first good) = 7/10. P(second good | first good) = 6/9 = 2/3.

summary: P(A | B) = P(A and B) / P(B). Read the question carefully — the condition is after the vertical bar.
Without replacement: after each draw, reduce the total count and adjust the relevant group count.
To find P(at least one...), use the complement: 1 - P(none...).
In a two-way table or Venn diagram, the conditional event restricts you to one row, column, or circle.
